messages.author: Thomas
messages.text: I have a Waveshare SX1262 Pico hat, which works well. For possible use as a battery-powered repeater, I looked into power saving and found I could reduce the current by 3 x (32 mA down to 9.5 mA), with some fairly straightforward changes. Before I start trying to write a PR - is there any ongoing discussion about this sort of thing elsewhere? I think the ESP32 variants have a big power saving in the nightlies. Is there interest in implementing these in the codebase? I think these changes are pretty safe and uncontroversial, but there may be some other experience or opinion to consider. Details of the savings: Receive: at 5 volts Vsys, the Pico current consumption is: 32 mA - Release firmware 13.0 mA - Reduce clocks to 18 MHz (48 MHz if USB is connected), enable PFM mode of the Pico SMPS. 12.5 mA - Added __asm volatile ("wfi"); to the end of Dispatcher::loop(). This halts the CPU core every millisecond until the next SysTick interrupt. Needs to be done carefully to avoid breaking other architectures. 11.2 mA - Disable the USB PLL entirely and route the system clock to the ADC peripheral 10.7 mA - Add -D SX126X_REGULATOR=RADIOLIB_SX126X_REGULATOR_DCDC (requires the inductor to be fitted for the built-in buck converter, which it is on this board) 9.5 mA - physically removed the super bright red Power On LED Transmit current drops from 99 mA to 85 mA - for this repeater at home so far, transmit time is only 30 minutes per day, so on average less than 2 mA extra. These are easy firmware changes (apart from the LED), and bring this board down to compete with the lowest power repeaters out there.
messages.content_html:
I have a Waveshare SX1262 Pico hat, which works well. For possible use as a battery-powered repeater, I looked into power saving and found I could reduce the current by 3 x (32 mA down to 9.5 mA), with some fairly straightforward changes. Before I start trying to write a PR - is there any ongoing discussion about this sort of thing elsewhere? I think the ESP32 variants have a big power saving in the nightlies. Is there interest in implementing these in the codebase? I think these changes are pretty safe and uncontroversial, but there may be some other experience or opinion to consider. Details of the savings: Receive: at 5 volts Vsys, the Pico current consumption is:
  • 32 mA - Release firmware
  • 13.0 mA - Reduce clocks to 18 MHz (48 MHz if USB is connected), enable PFM mode of the Pico SMPS.
  • 12.5 mA - Added __asm volatile ("wfi"); to the end of Dispatcher::loop(). This halts the CPU core every millisecond until the next SysTick interrupt. Needs to be done carefully to avoid breaking other architectures.
  • 11.2 mA - Disable the USB PLL entirely and route the system clock to the ADC peripheral
  • 10.7 mA - Add -D SX126X_REGULATOR=RADIOLIB_SX126X_REGULATOR_DCDC (requires the inductor to be fitted for the built-in buck converter, which it is on this board)
  • 9.5 mA - physically removed the super bright red Power On LED Transmit current drops from 99 mA to 85 mA - for this repeater at home so far, transmit time is only 30 minutes per day, so on average less than 2 mA extra. These are easy firmware changes (apart from the LED), and bring this board down to compete with the lowest power repeaters out there.
messages.reactions_html: 3

messages.author: Thomas
messages.text: here's the PR: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1539 The 9.5 mA is measured at Vsys = 5 V. On a 1s battery it will be closer to 13 or 14 mA.
messages.content_html: here's the PR: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1539 The 9.5 mA is measured at Vsys = 5 V. On a 1s battery it will be closer to 13 or 14 mA.
embeds_html:
power saving: enable DCDC, reduce clock speeds and voltage by thoma...
Power saving for the waveshare Pico hat. In my tests this reduces the Rx current into the pico Vsys from 32 mA to about 10 mA, and Tx current from 99 mA to 85 mA. (Measured at Vsys = 5 V. At 3.6 V ...

messages.author: Thomas
messages.text: Hmm, 8 seconds is a long time to stay alive and then crash. It's probably wired correctly otherwise it wouldn't work at all. Power problems? The SX1262 takes 150 mA, a 100 uF capacitor might help? Does the RP2040-zero have the same amount of flash memory as the regular Pico? Could the FW be writing to the filesystem and crashing when it runs out? Wire integrity? Breadboard wires are OK but for high speed SPI the slightest scratch or crackle might crash it. Try wiggling all the breadboard wires gently during the 8 seconds, see if it crashes sooner. On a breadboard there's a small chance of interference between the RF and the jumper wires. Try terminating the antenna connector with a load, or a long cable to the antenna, instead of using a short antenna right next to the board? If you don't have either of those, just hold the antenna firmly in your hand, and press your hand against the grounded case of the RA62, to reduce the radiated RF, and see if that changes anything. OR put the antenna right next to all of the breadboard wires, see if that makes it worse. Keep the jumpers short. Connect several ground wires, not just one.
messages.content_html: Hmm, 8 seconds is a long time to stay alive and then crash. It's probably wired correctly otherwise it wouldn't work at all. Power problems? The SX1262 takes 150 mA, a 100 uF capacitor might help? Does the RP2040-zero have the same amount of flash memory as the regular Pico? Could the FW be writing to the filesystem and crashing when it runs out? Wire integrity? Breadboard wires are OK but for high speed SPI the slightest scratch or crackle might crash it. Try wiggling all the breadboard wires gently during the 8 seconds, see if it crashes sooner. On a breadboard there's a small chance of interference between the RF and the jumper wires. Try terminating the antenna connector with a load, or a long cable to the antenna, instead of using a short antenna right next to the board? If you don't have either of those, just hold the antenna firmly in your hand, and press your hand against the grounded case of the RA62, to reduce the radiated RF, and see if that changes anything. OR put the antenna right next to all of the breadboard wires, see if that makes it worse. Keep the jumpers short. Connect several ground wires, not just one.

messages.author: ronniedude
messages.text: Similar query. Do I have to use the waveshare lora module with the default meshcore pico firmware or could I use a breadboard and wire up any other sx1262 lora module with an SPI interface? For example using a Pimoroni Tiny RP2040 ( https://shop.pimoroni.com/products/tiny-2040) + the seeedstudio lora module meant for the XIAO ESP32 & nrf5's ( https://www.seeedstudio.com/Wio-SX1262-for-XIAO-p-6379.html) (edited)
messages.content_html: Similar query. Do I have to use the waveshare lora module with the default meshcore pico firmware or could I use a breadboard and wire up any other sx1262 lora module with an SPI interface? embeds_html:
Tiny 2040
A postage stamp sized RP2040 development board with a USB-C connection, perfect for portable projects, wearables, and embedding into stuff.

Wio-SX1262 for XIAO
This is a high-performance LoRa module specifically designed for the XIAO series development boards. Based on the Semtech SX1262 chip, it supports global LoRa bands (862-930MHz). Combined with the powerful computing capabilities of the XIAO series, it provides developers with a compact and efficient LoRa communication solution. It is suitable fo...

messages.reply_html:
Mike's Allotment
I've been continuing with playing with the RP2040-Zero + HT-RA62 combo and have made quite a bit of progress, however I'm seeing problems with config changes not being saved and all changes are lost after reboot (both Repeater and Companion USB). Has anyone else seen similar problems with the RP2040? I'm aware that the RP2040-Zero only has 260K SRAM and the existing Xiao, Waveshare, rasp-pico rp2040 variants are the only variants to use LittleFS (other platforms use SPIFFS or InternalFS/CustomFS). Any thoughts pls? (edited)
